ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured donut; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared snack foods is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium flavoured donut weighing approximately 75 g is a typical single serving consumed as a snack or breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured donut — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for flavoured donut (CR ID: F11100005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cake-type glazed doughnut (FDC ID 174987) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Doughnut, cake type — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 76 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cake-type doughnut (glucose reference = 100). A flavoured/glazed donut is compositionally analogous to a cake-type doughnut — refined flour base, high sugar glaze, deep-fried — making this the closest defensible GI anchor. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = 76 × (46.05 × 0.75) / 100 = 76 × 34.54 / 100 ≈ 26.2, rounded to 26.4 using serving size of 75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (22.64 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.18 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium at 304 mg/100 g adds direct pressor load; saturated fat contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid flavoured donuts; the combination of 22.64 g sugar/100 g (exceeding the solid-food sugar gate of >10 g/100 g with fibre <2 g/100 g), 304 mg sodium/100 g, and high saturated fat creates compounding cardiovascular risk through insulin-mediated sodium retention and arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 22.64 g/100 g exceeds the diabetes hard gate (>20 g/100 g); GI of 76 (high) combined with 46.05 g carbohydrate/100 g produces a very high glycaemic load of 26.4 per serving; only 1.18 g dietary fibre/100 g provides negligible glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid flavoured donuts entirely; with sugar at 22.64 g/100 g, a GI of 76, and a glycaemic load of 26.4 per 75 g serving, this food causes significant postprandial glucose spikes, and the trans fat content further worsens insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~8 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~1.5 g/100 g) from deep-frying in partially hydrogenated oils raise LDL cholesterol and lower HDL; high sugar drives triglyceride synthesis; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote endothelial dysfunction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid flavoured donuts; the estimated trans fat of 1.5 g/100 g and saturated fat of 8 g/100 g directly raise atherogenic LDL cholesterol, while the high sugar load (22.64 g/100 g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia, collectively representing a high-risk lipid and glycaemic profile for CVD management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (~1.5 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory; deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation; high sugar (22.64 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid flavoured donuts entirely; the combination of estimated trans fat (1.5 g/100 g), high saturated fat (8 g/100 g), deep-frying-generated AGEs, and 22.64 g sugar/100 g triggers multiple pro-inflammatory pathways that directly exacerbate joint inflammation and disease activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A flavoured donut is a deep-fried sweet dough ring made from refined wheat flour, sugar, eggs, milk, and yeast, then coated or filled with various flavoured glazes, icings, or toppings such as chocolate, strawberry, vanilla, or sprinkles. The dough is leavened, shaped into a ring or filled ball, and submerged in hot oil until golden, producing a soft, pillowy interior with a slightly crisp exterior. The glaze or icing adds a sweet, sticky coating that contributes significantly to the sugar content. Nutritionally, a flavoured donut is energy-dense at 478 kcal/100 g, with high fat (30 g/100 g, including approximately 8 g saturated fat and an estimated 1.5 g trans fat from deep-frying), high carbohydrate (46 g/100 g), and very high sugar (22.64 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is low at 1.18 g/100 g, and sodium is moderate at 304 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index is high (approximately 76), producing a glycaemic load of approximately 26 per 75 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (sugar exceeds 20 g/100 g hard gate, high GI), hypertension (high sugar with low fibre drives insulin-mediated sodium retention), cardiovascular disease (trans fat and saturated fat raise LDL), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs and trans fat are pro-inflammatory). Donuts are widely consumed as a snack or breakfast item across Western and international food cultures, and are available in commercial bakeries, fast-food chains, and convenience stores globally, including in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Can a diabetic eat a donut?
How many calories in a flavoured donut?
Is a glazed donut high in sugar?
Can I eat a donut if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a donut?
Is a donut high in fat?
How much sugar is in a donut?
Is a donut suitable for someone with heart disease?
What is the difference between a donut and a doughnut?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F11100005) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central closest analogue (cake-type glazed doughnut, FDC ID 174987). Trans fat estimated from published literature on deep-fried dough products. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cake-type doughnut. Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (8.0 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cake-type glazed doughnut (FDC ID 174987) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. Trans fat (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from published literature on deep-fried dough products using partially hydrogenated vegetable oils; this value is not directly reported by HPB and should be treated as an approximation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this specific flavoured donut formulation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 76 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cake-type doughnut; no direct GI measurement for flavoured donut was identified. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; donuts may contain lard-based shortening in some formulations — consumers should verify with the specific manufacturer. 6. The dietary tag 'plant_based' from the input record has been removed as donuts typically contain eggs and milk and are not plant-based; this appears to be an input data error.
